# スイッチからCPU (Switch → CPU)

> A free, browser-only game where you build digital logic from a single NAND
> gate all the way up to a working CPU — a modern remake of nandgame /
> nand-to-tetris. Bilingual (Japanese / English). No accounts, no ads.

This is a single-page app with hash-based routing (`#<levelId>`, e.g.
`#not`, `#and`, `#cpu`, `#sandbox`). Each "page" is a puzzle: wire up parts
on a grid so the circuit matches a target truth table (or, for memory
levels, a target timing sequence).

## Solving puzzles with an AI (operation API)

The page exposes a structured operation API at `window.karakuri` for an
in-browser AI agent (e.g. Claude in Chrome) to read and solve puzzles
precisely, instead of inferring from screenshots. Everything is JSON.

Discovery: on load the app prints a console banner ("operation API ready —
call karakuri.help()") and sets `document.documentElement` attribute
`data-karakuri-api`. If you are an agent, prefer calling this API over
clicking the SVG editor — it is faster and exact.

Discover it at runtime:

- `karakuri.help()` — the full method list and a worked example
- `karakuri.problem()` — the CURRENT puzzle as data: goal, target truth
  table (or timing sequence), available parts and their pin names, the
  grid size, and the par (reference gate count)
- `karakuri.state()` — the current circuit: placed parts with pin values,
  wires, cost, and the live input values
- `karakuri.verify()` — run the checker; returns `{ pass, oscillated, rows,
  failing }` with the exact failing input rows

Act:

- `karakuri.goto(idOrIndex)` — load a level (also updates the URL)
- `karakuri.place(kind, x, y, chipId?)` — drop a part, returns `{ id }`.
  `kind` ∈ `nand | chip | dff | high | low | nmos | pmos`; for a built chip,
  pass its `chipId` (e.g. `"NOT"`, `"AND"`, `"MUX"`).
- `karakuri.wire(fromId, fromPin, toId, toPin)` — connect two pins.
- `karakuri.setInput(name, 0|1)`, `karakuri.move(id, x, y)`,
  `karakuri.remove(id)`, `karakuri.clear()`, `karakuri.hint(n)`,
  `karakuri.grid(dCols, dRows)`.

Notes:

- `state().cost` is the CURRENT board; `state().bestCost` is your saved
  record — they can differ. Only `verify()` advances scoring/solved.
- Memory levels (`kind: "sequential"` — SR/D latch, register, counter,
  CPU) are checked against `problem().sequence`: ordered `{in, expected}`
  steps applied without resetting state, so a `clk` 0→1 edge latches.
  Build the circuit and call `verify()`; it replays the whole sequence.
  DFF pins are `d` (in), `clk` (in), `q` (out).
- `place()`/`wire()` return `{error, ...}` (with `validIds`/`validPins`)
  on bad input instead of throwing, so you can self-correct.

Coordinates are integer grid cells: `x` = column (0 = left, inputs live
there), `y` = row. Outputs sit on the right edge. Interface I/O is
pre-placed and locked: an input named `X` has instance id `in_X` and you
drive from its pin `y`; an output named `Y` has id `out_Y` and you feed its
pin `x`. Get exact ids/pins from `karakuri.state()`.

Worked example — build NOT = NAND(X, X):

    const { id } = karakuri.place("nand", 6, 4);
    karakuri.wire("in_X", "y", id, "a");
    karakuri.wire("in_X", "y", id, "b");
    karakuri.wire(id, "y", "out_Y", "x");
    karakuri.verify();   // { pass: true, ... }

## The ladder of levels

Transistor (switch) → NAND → NOT/AND/OR/XOR/NOR/XNOR → half/full adder →
SR & D latch (memory) → MUX & register → ALU → program counter → a tiny
accumulator CPU. Plus a free-build sandbox and bonus transistor levels.
Each solved level becomes a reusable chip in your palette.

## Notes

- Progress (built chips, best scores, in-progress circuits) is stored in
  the browser's localStorage. There is no server and no leaderboard.
- Solutions are scored by part count (NANDs, or transistors on switch
  levels); a `★` means you hit or beat par.
